titleStmt.title: A Transcription of John in Codex Sangallensis 1394
VL16
The New Testament
The Gospel according to John
john
titleStmt.funder: The Arts and Humanities Research Council

publisher.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing
University of Birmingham

availability.p: Licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0): available for re-use provided attribution is made to the original creators (the IGNTP and any named participants) and an indication is given of any changes made. See further .

msItem.title: Fragmentum Euangelii Secundum Iohannem (ex Quattuor Evangeliis)
A fragment of John, from a manuscript of the four Gospels in order Mt Jo Lc Mc

surrogates.bibl: Black and white photographs in ITSEE, supplied by Vetus Latina-Institut.
Images of the Stiftsbibliothek fragments of the Synoptic Gospels (but not John) are online at http://www.e-codices.unifr.ch/en/csg/1394/

listBibl.bibl: Wordsworth, J., Sanday, W. and White, H.J., Portions of the Gospels according to St Mark and St Matthew. (Oxford Old Latin Biblical Texts 2). Oxford 1886.
B. Bischoff, in Miscellanea Giovanni Mercati, Vol. 1, Rome, 1946, pp. 420-4

projectDesc.p: This transcription was made by the Verbum Project as part of an online and printed edition of the Old Latin versions of John between 2003 and 2010.

editorialDecl.p: This transcription covers ff. 10-11. These contain John 19:13-17, 24-42. The rest of the manuscript is in various places; the bulk at St Gall Stiftsbibliothek 1394.The transcription includes foliation, lineation, all punctuation and abbreviations. Although the editors agree on the readings of the extant part of Folio 10, they differ in the restoration. Our edition has followed Bischoff's reconstruction, while supplying the differences between the editions as footnotes. It should also be noted that while White regularly finds the first 1-3 letters illegible on the left hand side of Folio 11, they are quite clear in the plates; it seems they were still folded over when White consulted the manuscript, but have subsequently been flattened. White was, however, better placed than the present editors to judge the relative antiquity of hands and correctors, and we also reproduce his transcription of the High German glosses as a footnote. The initial transcription file was a plain text file for use with COLLATE. Further information about the procedures followed may be found in the electronic edition at
This second edition of the file was converted to XML by Catherine Smith using the project's Python conversion scripts 1.0.
The XML specifications used are version 1.5 of the IGNTP Guidelines available at

revisionDesc.change: Adjustment of transcription to version 1.5 of the XML by Hugh Houghton.
Reconversion of the revised plain text file using Python scripts to version 1.4 of the XML by Catherine Smith and manual checking.
Revision of the plain text file to include new fragments from Gamper 2012 by Hugh Houghton.
Hyphen entity replaced by lb attribute break="no" by Hugh Houghton
Changes made in order to display properly with VLI.xsl transformation by Hugh Houghton
Transcription converted to the TEI XML schema and corrected for parsing by Hugh Houghton

body.note: StiASG Fragment Bl recto, Bf2 recto

ab.w:
po
StiASG Fragment Bl verso, Fragment Bf2 versoNote that some of the characters on this page which are no longer visible on p.110 are visible as offset on p. 113
test

ab.note: There is an original hole in the parchment in the middle of nihil
StiASG Fragment Bb verso and offset from Bc on p. 119
StiASG Fragment Aa recto plus offset

ab.w:
mun
StiASG Fragment Aa verso
do

ab.note: Note in left margin, 15/16th century: Offic. curia const(antiensis) p(u)bl(ic)o in s(an)cto gallo notum facimus q(uod) dn¬s n(oste)r cpo f.lio

There are Old High German glosses above the next seven lines apart from proper nouns. The reading is: aft(er) deisu so pat (pilatum ioseph ab arimathia) der uuas disco (iesu) taucono so duruh forahtun iudeono
